risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The remaining 297 definite Meniere's patients were randomly assigned through computer-generated block randomization organized by the clinical study section at Osaka Rosai Hospital to one of four treatment groups." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence using block randomization, which is a proper random method for sequence generation.

Quote: "The remaining 297 definite Meniere's patients were randomly assigned through computer-generated block randomization organized by the clinical study section at Osaka Rosai Hospital to one of four treatment groups." Comment: This reports random sequence generation and that allocation was organized centrally by a study section, but it does not explicitly state a concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation used for enrolment, SNOSE, or identical containers) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrolment. Therefore allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This clinical study is a randomized-controlled and open-label trial with four arms including a control group." Comment: The trial explicitly states it was open-label, so participants and personnel were not blinded. Because the main outcomes were vertigo control and hearing improvement over 24 months, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence co-interventions, reporting, adherence, and outcome assessment behavior, so performance bias is high overall.

Quote: "This clinical study is a randomized-controlled and open-label trial with four arms including a control group." and "All patients were followed up for vertigo and hearing at least once a month..." and "Vertigo ... was regarded as a Meniere’s vertigo attack... Hearing was measured by pure tone audiometry... Questionnaires included the Self-rating Depression Scale (SDS) and Stress Response Scale-18 (SRS-18)." Comment: The study was explicitly open-label, with no report that the outcome assessor was blinded. For the main outcomes, vertigo control and questionnaires rely on patient report/self-report, so knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. Hearing was measured by audiometry, but because the trial was open-label and the reported outcomes include subjective/self-reported components, detection bias is likely for the outcomes relevant to this review.

Quote: "By the final evaluation 24 months after start of treatment, 70 cases remained in G-I, 70 in G-II, 63 in G-III, and 60 in G-IV (i.e. 4 in G-I, 5 in G-II, 11 in G-III and 14 in G-IV were excluded)." Comment: Attrition was reported and was modest overall (23/297 randomized, about 7.7%), with all groups retaining most participants. Although exclusions were somewhat higher in the sleeping-in-darkness and tube groups, the paper states the reason was non-compliance (<75% adherence) or tube removal, which is plausibly related to protocol adherence rather than the outcomes themselves. Overall, this level of missing data is unlikely to have biased the results materially.
